topic: Gravity I — Why Things Fall Down

bee_quiz.question: The invisible force that pulls every object with mass toward every other object with mass is called what?
The curving path of the Moon as it travels around Earth is called what?
The amount of stuff (matter) inside an object is called its what?
The metric system unit used to measure how much mass an object has is called what?
Roughly what fraction of Earth's gravity does the Moon have?

choices.c: one out of three hundred thousand

bee_quiz.question: An apple falling, rain falling, and a child sliding down a slide are all examples of which force at work?
Without air, a feather and a hammer dropped from the same height will land when?

choices.a: the hammer first because it is heavier
choices.c: the feather first because it floats

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Heavier objects fall faster — kids assume mass affects falling speed, but in a vacuum all objects fall at the same rate.

bee_quiz.question: Astronauts on the ISS appear to float not because gravity is gone but because of what?

choices.a: gravity is much weaker that high up
choices.d: they and their station are falling around Earth together

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Kids think astronauts float because there is no gravity in space — but the ISS is only 400 km up; they're actually in free-fall.

bee_quiz.question: On the Moon, your body has the same matter as on Earth but you weigh less. The thing that changed is what?

choices.c: the Moon's gravity is weaker than Earth's
choices.d: the Moon has less air resistance

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Kids confuse mass with weight — moving to the Moon doesn't change mass, only weight (which depends on gravity).

bee_quiz.question: From STRONGEST gravity to weakest, the chapter compares Sun, Jupiter, Earth, and Moon in what order?
A roller-coaster drop, a bottle falling out of your hand, and astronauts in the ISS are all examples of what?
Earth completes one full orbit around the Sun about how often?
Which of these is **NOT** something the chapter says gravity does?

choices.a: holds the air around our planet
choices.d: makes things invisible to our eyes

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is **NOT** true about the difference between MASS and WEIGHT?

choices.a: mass changes when you visit the Moon while weight stays the same
choices.b: mass measures matter; weight measures gravity's pull on it
choices.c: weight depends on which planet you are on
choices.d: mass does not change between Earth and the Moon

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is **NOT** what causes air to slow a falling object?

choices.a: the surface area of the object

word_cards.definition: An invisible pulling force that exists between every two things that have matter.
word_cards.example: When you drop a pencil, this force pulls it down toward the centre of the Earth.

word_cards.definition: The amount of stuff (matter) packed inside an object — does not change if you go to the Moon.
word_cards.example: A 30 kg child has 30 kg of body whether they are on Earth, the Moon, or floating in space.

word_cards.definition: How hard gravity is pulling on an object — changes depending on which planet or moon you are on.
word_cards.example: A 30 kg child weighs about 30 kg on Earth but only 5 kg on the Moon, even though their body is the same.

word_cards.definition: A push or a pull that can make something move, slow down, speed up, or change direction.
word_cards.example: Throwing a ball is a push; pulling a wagon is a pull; both are this.

word_cards.definition: A curving path that one object follows as it goes around another, larger object.
word_cards.example: The Moon traces a giant circle around Earth once each month — that is the path it follows.

word_cards.definition: When an object is falling and the only force acting on it is gravity.
word_cards.example: On a roller-coaster drop, for a split second nothing else holds you up — that is what astronauts feel all the time.

word_cards.definition: The force of the air pushing back on an object as it moves through it, slowing it down.
word_cards.example: A flat sheet of paper drifts down slowly, but a crumpled ball drops fast because there is less surface for the air to push on.

word_cards.definition: A space with no air or any other matter inside it.
word_cards.example: Astronauts on the Moon proved that a feather and a hammer fall at the same time when there is no air around them.

word_cards.definition: The metric unit scientists use to measure how much matter is inside an object.
word_cards.example: A small dog weighing about 5 of these is the same size of body whether it is on Earth or on the Moon.

word_cards.definition: The famous English scientist who first explained how gravity works.
word_cards.example: An apple is said to have fallen near him, and he wondered if the same pull worked on the Moon — and he was right.

_self_check.instrument_or_unit_reason: Chapter mentions kg (e.g. Earth's mass in kg, weight on different bodies). Q4 tests the kilogram unit.
